data.title: Kinsale Capital: Strong Business Fundamentals Support Upgrade To Strong Buy At Lower Price
data.description:
A Quick Recap of My Prior Article

I am upgrading Kinsale Capital Group (KNSL) from Buy to Strong Buy primarily because while business fundamentals of the company remain intact, its stock price has declined from around $400 to around $330 today, mostly due to souring market sentiments.

In my December 2025 article on Kinsale Capital Group, Inc., I demonstrated, through both quantitative measures such as growth rates, workforce productivity, and profitability ratios and qualitative features such as its properly aligned incentives for employees and agents alike, that Kinsale operates with a superb business model delivering consistently high profitability and growth rates, subject to cyclical trends, a combination that is rarely seen in the insurance industry. The stock was traded at $375 or so then.

As illustrated in my article, operating with such distinct and probably enduring competitive advantages, over the prior three- and five-year periods, Kinsale grew its revenue at roughly twice the pace of peers such as Arch, Markel, and Berkley. That is an uncommon feat for an insurer, where many competitors were struggling to grow at all.

The company’s workforce of about 800 employees has demonstrated an unusual level of productivity. Empowered by its internally developed IT systems, each Kinsale employee has been, on average, handling roughly 180 policy renewals and originations per year, resulting in an expense ratio (employee compensation as a percentage of total premium revenue) of the low 20%, compared to 30–40% for many competitors.

This level of productivity further enhances Kinsale's advantage from operating in the highly fragmented and less regulated Excess and Surplus insurance niche market, where insurers have more pricing power.

Most importantly, Kinsale keeps underwriting authority in-house rather than delegating it to brokers, and it compensates employees based on the profitability of the long-term policies they write rather than the volume of premiums they generate. In plain English, employees are paid to price risks appropriately and issue only smart policies to reduce its payout ratio (the amount paid to insurance claims as a percentage of premiums received), not to be busy generating top lines at the expense of the long-term bottom line.

Such underwriting discipline is an anchor for long-term success, and it is very difficult for established large competitors to replicate, with Chubb (see my article on it here) being a true exception, as its corporate culture has been so similarly anchored for its long-term stability and growth.

At the end, Kinsale has consistently reported a combined ratio (the aggregate of expense ratio and payout ratio) in the low 70s, whereas most publicly traded large insurance companies struggle at high 80s and low 90s during hard market cycles and edge toward 100% during soft market cycles.

What the Latest Earnings Call Actually Shows

The latest earnings call (see its full transcript here) in mid-February, 2026, revealed Kinsale has been behaving cautiously in a softer insurance market environment, but its business fundamentals remain strong despite the headwind facing the entire insurance market.

During the fourth quarter of 2025, Kinsale grew its operating earnings per share by 26% and its net income by 27%. Its industry-leading combined ratio remained strong at 71.7%, and its return on equity was approximately 26%. Book value per share increased by 33% year over year, and float grew by 23%.

What did slow down was premium growth due to the continued softening trend of the entire insurance sector. Its gross written premiums increased by only 1.8% in the quarter. However, management made it clear that this slowdown was largely driven by the commercial property segment, which has become intensely competitive. When that segment is excluded, the rest of the business continued to grow at approximately 10% to 13%.

Management also made it clear that they are not willing to chase growth at the expense of profitability. They emphasized that they continue to target returns in the low 20% range and are willing to slow growth in order to maintain underwriting discipline, which is consistent with my understanding of its long-term strategies and competitive advantages.

This is a sign that the company is acutely aware of the insurance market cycle it is in but chooses to stick to its guns to preserve its long-term profitability and competitive advantage.

However, the stock declined from around $400 to around $375 in response to the earnings call. It recovered to the $390s range by the end of February 2026 but then resumed its decline to around $330 today, apparently in response to the market jittery caused by the war in the Persian Gulf.

KNSL Valuation In Light of Stock Price Decline

If you look at its valuation table below (adopted from Seeking Alpha), whether it is measured against its earnings or sales, the stock of Kinsale still commands a modest amount premium, about 10% to 50% depending on the measure, or overvaluation (the semantics really depend on your perspectives) over its peers, except that it is undervalued if measured against its free cash flow by about 23%. The stock is valued roughly twice as high as those of its peers as measured by its book value, and that probably is due to the fact that Mr. Market is expecting it to rapidly grow its “float” to catch up.

However, if you compare its valuation against its own five-year average, it is now undervalued by 30%-50%. This is yet another unmistakable sign that the entire insurance industry is going through a softer cycle, as KNSL’s stock price has come down from the low $500s last summer to the range of $370-$400 during the winter to today's $330 or so despite its continued growth in earnings and revenues. By the way, this is also a cautionary note about the risks of buying cyclical stocks at high prices when the relevant sectors are booming.

Kinsale's Growth Trajectories

Let's review its growth trajectories in the table below (adopted from Seeking Alpha).

Its YOY growth rate, whether it is for revenue or earnings, has slowed down considerably (roughly by half) from its rolling 5-year average. However, its revenue growth YOY is still about twice as high as its sector median, and its revenue growth for the next 12 months is projected to be slightly ahead of its peers. Its earnings growth YOY, as measured by EBITDA at 22.6% and EPS Diluted at 21.8%, are still more than 94% and 56% higher than its sector median figures, respectively.

However, analysts all seem to be more pessimistic for Kinsale than the entire sector for the same growth metrics for the next 12 months, as they project Kinsale's EBITDA growth will grow at only 7% as compared to the sector's 11.7%, and its ESP diluted will grow at only 11.4% as compared to the sector's 12.8%. Then, the analyst community takes a U-turn by projecting a 15% 3-5 year CAGR of Kinsale’s EPS, compared more favorably to the sector's 12%.

I usually do not assign undue weight to analysts' projections. However, to the extent that those figures collectively have some value, the company's business fundamentals, including its long-term growth runway, appear to continue to lead its peers, which should largely justify its moderately higher valuation.

Last but not least, Kinsale has only about $225 million in debt. Relative to its $167 million cash position and its $7.5 billion capital, investors do not need to worry about its liquidity, which becomes more relevant when there is an industry downturn or macroeconomic turbulence.

The Downgrade and the Disconnect

As reported here, Jefferies downgraded the stock to an underperform or sell-equivalent rating with a price target of $312 from its previous target of $392, citing growth deceleration, potential margin pressure, and valuation concerns.

This downgrade may be “correct” or at least reasonable for investors with a short-term horizon, as the stock price may continue to slide before the soft insurance market bottoms out.

However, the downgrade occurred after the stock had declined materially, roughly 25% from its recent highs around $400. The analyst was effectively saying that the business fundamentals of the company had deteriorated by the same order of magnitude.

That conclusion feels reactive and mechanical. When the business fundamentals and therefore intrinsic value of an enterprise remain largely intact, the fact that its stock price has declined by as much as 25% or so thanks to the cyclical downturn of the sector it operates in should be a reason to upgrade the stock, not to downgrade it, as the stock now represents a better value proposition, at least that should be true for long-term investors.

A Bipolar Stock Market

The current market environment has a somewhat bipolar quality to it. On one side, there are companies, the haves of the stock market, with strong narratives, particularly in areas like artificial intelligence, where growth is expected and often taken for granted. These companies continue to command high valuations and take up a higher and higher portion of the entire stock market's valuation.

On the other side are the have-nots, the S&P 490 or so, some of which are quietly delivering strong performance but seeing their stocks fall well within the bear market territory. Kinsale falls into this category. To the extent their sector, whether it's homebuilding or insurance, faces any cyclical headwinds, their stock prices may have been disproportionately punished.

I do not mean to imply that the concerns about the insurance cycle or macroeconomic conditions are unfounded. The industry is indeed moving into a softer phase, and pricing pressure is real. Growth is likely to moderate, and margins may face some headwinds over time, and the global and US economies do face geopolitical risks, fiscal deficits, inflation, and other risks. For those reasons, investors should not assume that the current price of Kinsale stock represents the bottom. The cycle may take time to play out, and it is really hard to time the bottom of the market.

However, this is where and when we can selectively and incrementally build long positions in solid companies whose stocks are priced around or below their intrinsic values, so long as we do not see any structural or fundamental deterioration of their actual businesses. It is not catching the proverbial falling knife nor buying on the dip for the sake of buying on the dip.

Risks to My Thesis

There are, of course, risks to my thesis. The insurance market may remain soft for longer than expected, leading to continued pricing pressure and slower growth. In addition, while the stock is cheaper than before, further multiple compression is possible if growth slows more than anticipated and the soft market lasts longer. Finally, although Kinsale has demonstrated strong underwriting discipline, no insurer is immune to unexpected losses, litigation trends, or adverse regulatory developments.

These risks are inherent in the business and must be accepted by any investor in the sector.

Conclusion

The underlying business fundamentals and competitive advantages of Kinsale are not deteriorating, although the company is going through a soft cycle together with the rest of the entire insurance sector. It is adapting to a softer market environment while producing strong profitability and returns.

At the same time, the stock price has declined by about 25%. In my view, while such price decline reflects the insurance sector's short-term trend and the many clouds over the stock market, it has been excessive.

For the reasons above, I am upgrading KNSL to Strong Buy from the previous modest Buy rating. Back in December 2025, I was modestly concerned about its valuation, and such concern has been substantially mitigated. So, I will continue my dollar average approach to increase my holding in Kinsale as the current valuation provides a more attractive entry point.

data.title: In War, Things Tire Or Break, StandardAero To The Rescue
data.description:
"If you can walk away from a landing, it's a good landing. If you can use the airplane the next day, it's an outstanding landing." -- Chuck Yeager

Let’s face it. The MRO (Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul) of aircraft engines and other key components is not the way to start a conversation with someone you just met and would like to impress. Even between two guys just having a beer together, unless they are both engineers, this is a sure way to have fewer friends.

But a $6 billion a year revenue stream from a company that is more than a century old (and recently revitalized), one that is the largest independent MRO firm in the country, and one that is about to enjoy a windfall from all the action already seen from the current administration, can stir even the most jaded investor’s cold heart. I therefore promise to include some history and maxims from pilots and mechanics along the way.

A Brief History of StandardAero

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------In 1898, Samuel Langley, physicist, professor of astronomy, and third secretary of the Smithsonian Institution, began experimenting with a heavier-than-air manned “aerodrome.” On October 3, 1903, he was ready to demonstrate it. Regrettably, it didn’t work. Days later, this editorial appeared: “…the flying machine that will really fly might be evolved by the combined and continuous efforts of mathematicians and mechanicians in from one million to ten million years…” -- New York Times

Two months later, bicycle mechanics Wilbur and Orville Wright developed and flew the first manned airplane.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The predecessor to StandardAero (SARO) was founded in 1911 in Winnipeg, Canada, as Standard Machine Works. Just before WWII, StandardAero was spun off and became fully independent in 1940.

StandardAero was purchased as part of a diversification push by the larger Federal Grain Company in September 1968 (later renamed Federal Industries Ltd.). By the 1970s, StandardAero was overhauling piston engines for airplanes and turboshaft engines for helicopters.

StandardAero was effectively sold four times more until, in 2004, private equity firm The Carlyle Group bought it, only to sell it three years later to Dubai Aerospace Enterprises. StandardAero’s headquarters were moved to Phoenix, Arizona.

SARO is still in Scottsdale today, but it keeps those home fires burning: in partnership with GE Aerospace, it operates the joint Testing, Research, and Development Centre (TRDC) in Winnipeg, which focuses specifically on essential icing certification and cold-weather testing for some of the world’s largest jet engines.

The corporate comings and goings over the years of StandardAero steadily acquiring other firms and being acquired itself could fill a page, and we would all be asleep.

Basically, SARO continued to expand its repertoire of jet engines and other key components, adding, in recent years, the H-60 Black Hawk, performing final assembly in the UK, and the F-16 and other advanced aircraft. Finally, just seventeen months ago, in October 2024, StandardAero became a public company and is now listed on the New York Stock Exchange.

“Flying is the second greatest thrill known to man. Landing is the first."

– attributed to John J. Nance

What Does StandardAero Really DO?

SARO is the largest independent MRO (again, Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul) in the United States. “Independent” means they are not owned or beholden to one aircraft manufacturer or one huge airline company. This means they are not competing with their customers by showing favoritism to one brand.

The US military has fabulous technical training and extensive activities to keep its MRO personnel busy and quite capable. But when a war comes along, the corps of personnel devoted to maintenance and repair (we call them “Maintainers”) is swamped. Just as we don't want to build a church for Easter Sunday, we don’t want so many brilliant (and expensively trained) men and women with nothing to do.

That’s where SARO enters the picture. It is an essential homegrown “ally” keeping the world's most-flown fighter jet, the F-16, and the workhorse (and fun to jump out of) C-130 operational until 2040 and beyond.

If that weren’t enough, StandardAero’s specialty has expanded from numerous older engines to the LEAP, which will be the dominant commercial engine for at least the next 20 years.

"There are three simple rules for making a smooth landing. Unfortunately, no one knows what they are." -- Unknown

The LEAP Engine? What the Heck is That?

The Leading Edge Aviation Propulsion engine is one of the most successful and technologically advanced commercial aviation jet engines in service today. It is lower-carbon and easier on fuel than all its predecessors.

It is built by CFM International, a 50/50 joint venture between GE Aerospace and France’s Safran Aircraft Engines.

The partners each do what they do best. GE is responsible for the high-pressure compressor, combustor, and high-pressure turbine. Safran handles the fan, gearbox, and low-pressure turbine.

The LEAP-1A engine competes head-to-head with Pratt & Whitney’s PW1100G. Both compete for the Airbus A320neo aircraft. LEAP 1B has no competitor. It is the sole supplier to the Boeing 737 MAX family of airplanes. LEAP-1C is built for the COMAC C919. It also has no competitor.

So what is the big deal about these LEAP engines? Among other things…

  • The fan blades are made from a 3D “Woven Carbon Fiber” composite that is lighter and more durable; lighter means less fuel, and more durable means less expensive maintenance.
  • The use of Ceramic Matrix composites in the high-pressure turbines. These “CMCs” can withstand temperatures much higher than those of traditional metal alloys, all the while weighing much less.
  • The fuel nozzles, which in previous versions of other engines required 20 separate parts welded together, are now 3D printed as a single piece. When it comes time to replace them, it takes way less time, and that means way less expense.

Yada, Yada, Yada. What Does This Have to Do with StandardAero?

"Airspeed, altitude, and brains. At least two are always required..."

Source: “The Pilot’s Trinity,” popularized by flight instructors like William K. Kershner, who wrote The Student Pilot’s Flight Manual.

While GE and Safran have MRO capabilities and facilities, other companies must meet significantly stricter requirements to be certified to maintain, repair, and overhaul these engines. It comes down to a small handful of airlines, and, yep, StandardAero leads the list.

From keeping F-16 fighters in the fight to mission-critical military transports, and more recently, helicopters as well, StandardAero is the engine that keeps aviation moving.

As the nation's largest independent MRO, operational readiness is baked into its thinking. We are not talking about Tinkertoys here. You want a hi-tech investment? The intricate engineering involved in maintaining, repairing, and overhauling fast-burner military jets, commercial airlines, helicopters, and private jets – yes, we haven’t mentioned that, but they are a top dog here, too! – “StandardAero is the Standard Bearer” for keeping airplanes where they belong: in the sky, not on the ground.

I have spoken mostly about the essential part of commercial, Bizjet, and military aircraft—the engines—but StandardAero is becoming a “one-stop shop" across the industry.

They now install and repair Avionics such as cockpit displays, flight controls, and navigation systems.

They provide maintenance for the airframe itself, the plane's body, including structural repairs and major modifications.

StandardAero builds the Auxiliary Power Units that provide power while the plane is on the ground.

SARO also has a large, specialized division that repairs individual parts rather than replacing them with expensive new ones. StandardAero’s “Repair-Rather-Than-Replace" technology substantially lowers costs for aircraft operators of all types.

SARO even gets into the interior of the planes, where they start fresh and redesign luxury jet interiors.

Speed, reliability, engineering excellence, and its role as the globe’s largest independent pure-play aerospace engine MRO place StandardAero at the top of the heap.

Valuation

Comparing SARO with its only true US independent competitor, AAR Corp. (AIR), StandardAero sells at a forward PE of about 19, while AAR is projected to be just below that. However, AAR has a $2.5 million market cap, and StandardAero's is more than 3.5 times that. In addition, I see no special catalyst that will significantly change AAR's immediate future revenue, whereas SARO has only this year begun to be involved with the LEAP engine programs. These, I believe, are a major catalyst.

There has been some recent insider selling, but let me put that in context:

Earlier this month, CEO Russell Wayne Ford sold 40,000 shares, but that was a Rule 10b5-1 sale under a plan adopted in August 2025. On the other hand, Independent Director Paul McElhinney bought 37,243 shares in the open market at about $26.41 per share. I interpret that buying with his own money as a sign that this lead director believes the current market price is an attractive entry point.

So is the entry price a good time to consider beginning a position? I think so. SARO is just beginning to generate meaningful revenue from the LEAP engine, and management expects these new endeavors to reach full profitability within the next 90 days.

Finally, SARO management seems to agree with me—the board has now authorized a $450M buyback. Clearly, they believe the stock is priced right, as well!

Risk Factors?

World peace could reign. People could stop flying. Bizjet owners could be shamed by their airplane’s carbon footprint into flying merely First Class on a commercial airline.

Since none of these are likely to occur in our lifetime, SARO could, more realistically, lose its compelling edge by hiring poorly. Or the OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) like GE and Safran could decide they would rather have the expense of establishing scores of maintenance sites around the world, as StandardAero already has.

Of course, there are competitors to consider: other large independent MROs include Germany’s Lufthansa Technik, AAR Corp., Singapore-based ST Engineering, and MTU Aero Engines. Against them, SARO could stumble and lose the current inbred capabilities it enjoys with GE, SAFRAN, the US military, and others.

Supply chain constraints that slow the current pace at which SARO serves its customers could occur, resulting in lower cash flow while waiting for parts.

And these days, there is always the threat of some sort of tariff tirade or a government shutdown. Of course, these would affect all competitors and all end users equally. (As they would, as has been shown most recently, every industry, every sector, and every one of us.)

StandardAero: the nation’s biggest airplane “mechanic.”

StandardAero is a $6 billion company (2025 revenues) with a 15.8% YOY growth profile. The company has over 5,000 commercial, military, and business aviation clients, who are served by 8,000 StandardAero specialists.

The runway for Standard Auto is wide and long. Every 400 hours an F-16 flies, it is taken in for a Phase Inspection. G-forces and supersonic speed take their toll on pilot and aircraft. Maintainers strip off over 100 panels to check for hairline structural cracks, hydraulic leaks, and engine wear that aren't visible during daily pre-flight checks.

Every 1,000 to 2,000 hours, an F-16 engine typically requires a "Hot Section Inspection" (HSI), during which high-heat components such as turbine blades are inspected or replaced. And every 3,000-4,000 hours of use, the jet goes to a "Depot"—a massive facility like those run by StandardAero.

When it comes to maintenance and engineering, every pilot knows that he is a partner with those on the ground who keep them flying.

"I really believe that more than half the battle of flying is not in the air, but on the ground in the hangar." — Beryl Markham, the first person to fly solo across the Atlantic from east to west (1936).

Of course, that doesn’t mean there isn’t some good-natured back-and-forth between these two partners. Here are some “pilot versus maintainers” -- possibly fictional–– discussions that flowed from pilot notes after a flight:

Pilot's Note: "Evidence of leak on right main landing gear." Mechanic's Response: "Evidence removed."

Pilot's Note: "Number 3 engine missing." Mechanic's Response: "Engine found on right wing after brief search."

Pilot: "Test flight OK, except auto-land very rough." Mechanic’s Response: “There is no auto-land on this aircraft.”

--- Primary Source: AviationHumor.net

The Bottom Line

StandardAero is unique. It is not owned by, nor does it have an exclusive single client in the airline business. It is not owned by, nor does it have an exclusive single client of an aircraft manufacturer. The entire aeronautical world is StandardAero’s to prevail or fail. I believe it will prevail.

Respectfully submitted,

Joseph L. Shaefer

data.title: Post-Iran Winners: Oil, Energy, And Israel
data.description:
Equities around the world continue to take it on the chin this March, with month-to-date performance coinciding with the beginning of the start of the war in Iran over the last weekend of February.

Below is a look at our asset class performance matrix featuring total returns for dozens of ETFs across domestic and international equities, sectors, currencies, commodities, and fixed income. For each ETF, we show year-to-date performance through February along with performance so far in March.

ETFs across asset classes are bleeding red this month with the exception of a few: bitcoin (IBIT), the energy sector (XLE), oil (USO) and other energy/ag commodities, and Israel (EIS). Of these month-to-date winners, oil, the agriculture commodities ETF (DBA), the energy sector, and Israel are the only ones that were also up year-to-date before the Iran war began.On the downside, mid-caps and small-caps have been some of the hardest-hit domestic index ETFs, with mid-cap value (IJJ) down the most at -8.1%. Every sector ETF is down this month except energy, with materials down the most at -12%.

Outside the US, country ETFs like France (EWQ), Germany (EWG), India (INDA), Italy (EWI), Japan (EWJ), and Mexico (EWW) are all down 10%+ since the war, while gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) have also gotten pummeled. Additionally, fixed income has not offered any safety due to inflation concerns brought about by the war. The 20+ Year Treasury ETF (TLT) is already down 5.2% since the bombings began.

Below is a look at the recent performance of more than 40 country stock market ETFs traded on US exchanges. On average, these country ETFs were up 9.7% through the first two months of 2026.

Since the end of February, though, they're down an average of 9.7%. Just a few weeks ago, it looked like 2026 was likely to be another year of outperformance for international markets.

Now that there's war in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz is unofficially closed for business, countries that heavily rely on energy imports are getting hit (with the exception of Israel). Along with Israel, Norway (ENOR) is the only other country ETF that's positive this month. As a reminder, Norway is one of the few non-Middle Eastern countries that's a net exporter of oil.

Six country ETFs are down 15%+ since the war began: Indonesia (EIDO), Vietnam (VNAM), South Korea (EWY), UAE (UAE), Peru (EPU), and South Africa (EZA).

Of the G7 countries, the US (SPY) is down the least, with a decline of 5.3%, followed by Canada (EWC) - down 8%, and the UK (EWU) - down 9.9%.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values Drop To Near 5-Year Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Zillow, the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends.

Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values continued to trend higher in February, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values continued to drop, remaining at their lowest level in nearly five years. Last month's ZHVI came in at $360,591, up 0.20% from the previous month and up 0.23% from one year ago. After adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.03% month-over-month and -2.86% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its “Zestimates,” its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag - like the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is at an all-time high.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 12.68% off their all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes.

This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI is “the most accurate and timely measures of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index Home Price Index. Zillow claims this is due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction.

Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

© Zillow.com

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are “substantially different from non-distressed sales” and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents “two very different market segments.” Eric Stubbs, Ph.D. at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales are a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do in fact affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information. ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Torrid Holdings Is Suffering Under The Weight Of GLP-1s
data.description:
Torrid Holdings Inc. (CURV) just reported Q4 '25 results.

The results were not good in my opinion, with comp sales down 10%, gross margins down 360 bps, and profitability deteriorating. What is more worrying is that this happened while the company leaned further into lower opening price points and promotions, which, in my opinion, points to very weak pricing power.

Management is guiding for another year of sales decline in FY26, with only modest adjusted EBITDA improvement after a year in which the company closed almost one-quarter of its stores.

The name may appear optically cheap at a market cap of roughly $160 million after the release, compared to cash-based earnings of ~$18/24 million next year based on guidance. Still, I do not think that is attractive considering the poor results, the difficult environment, and the fragility of retailer earnings under operating leverage. I maintain a Hold rating.

Q4 '25 results

Torrid posted comp sales down 10% in Q4 '25, while net sales fell 14%. The company closed 77 stores in the quarter and 151 stores through the year.

Management noted during the call that 460 bps of the comp decline came from the temporary pause of its shoe business (because of tariffs, as previously commented). Even adjusting for that, however, the result remains very weak.

Further, what I find more worrying is that sales declined sharply even while the company was lowering prices. Management said on the call that opening price points now represent about 30% of the total assortment and nearly 40% in stores. Even with lower prices, customers are still not responding.

Usually, one can defend volume with promotions or defend margins with pricing. When sales still fall despite cheaper opening price points, that suggests poor pricing power. A related trend affecting the company is obviously GLP-1 adoption.

Because of the pricing action, gross margins fell 360bps. Tariffs were not emphasized as a direct Q4 gross margin driver. Management has been talking about a roughly $50 million annual tariff headwind, and on this call again said there is tariff pressure in Q1. Because Torrid is simultaneously leaning into lower opening price points and remaining promotional, while facing higher tariffed prices, it makes sense for margins to fall.

One factor to remember is that lease expenses are recorded in CoGS, so not all of the gross margin deterioration is merchandise margin. Potentially a relevant factor is store deleveraging.

SG&A fell in dollar terms and leveraged 40 bps as a percentage of sales, helped by the closure of stores mentioned above. Store count ended the year at 483, down from 634 last year. That implies the company closed 24% of its stores during the year (with guidance for another 30 next year).

Against gross profit, SG&A deleveraged meaningfully. Gross profit fell by $22 million, almost twice the dollar decline in SG&A, and adjusted EBITDA dropped from ~$17 million last year to $5 million.

Weak guidance after a 'turnaround' year

For FY26, Torrid is guiding to net sales of $940 million to $960 million, down again from the $1 billion reported in FY25, and adjusted EBITDA of $65 million to $75 million, only modestly above the ~$64 million generated this year. Capital expenditures are guided to $8 million to $10 million. Management explained on the call it expects $40 million of additional cost savings from store optimization, but that this will be offset by lower gross profits on lower sales and by normalized incentive compensation (incentives for what, I wonder).

Valuation not attractive

The stock may look cheap on an adjusted EBITDA or cash-earnings basis, but I do not think it is.

Starting from the midpoint of FY26 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $70 million, and subtracting midpoint capex of $9 million, roughly $7 million of stock-based compensation (assuming as guided higher incentives versus this year), and about $31 million of annual interest expense, one gets to around $23 million of pretax cash earnings. Using a regular tax assumption, that is roughly $18 million after tax, but it is very probable that the company does not need to pay cash taxes because of D&A losses not included in the above calculation.

On a roughly $160 million market cap, that may look like about 7x to 9x cash earnings, which on the surface may sound attractive.

However, this is not a stable, steady-state business. Torrid is losing sales, closing stores, facing a difficult consumer backdrop, potentially facing a medical global trend as a headwind, and appears unable to convince customers even after lowering prices. Further, it is a retailer, meaning that even small misses on topline or gross margins can make guidance disappear under operating leverage.

In my opinion, Torrid Holdings Inc. stock is not attractive here. The results were poor, the outlook is weak, and operating leverage can decrease earnings quickly. I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Gran Tierra: Another Incident In The Wrong Direction
data.description:
Gran Tierra ( GTE) announced in a filing that four board members resigned. Shown below is one letter (because the four letters are very similar) that states the official "dispute reason" for the resignation.

At issue here's the resignation of four board members and, of course, the subsequent contraction of the board to the remaining five members. The 8K filing itself mentions that the investigation will continue. That would appear to mean that the original decision to terminate the inquiry has changed. A dispute of this magnitude, so as to cause nearly half of the board of directors to resign, should demand a very high level of transparency along with an explanation and the procedures going forward.

The resignations themselves and the filing mention there were not financial statement irregularities (at least at the time of the filing). But the sheer number of board members resigning would demonstrate a rather serious challenge. Specifically, sheer numbers of board members resigning to me indicate a fairly serious issue or issues that may or may not be related to the official reason stated in all the letters. Generally, that many resignations would (to me) require a thorough review of the situation that led to those resignations along with proper corrections. It's not every day that "that many board members" resign. More importantly, it's not every day that something like this happens in a company already financially stressed.

The last article had mentioned that this company has some serious financial issues. While those issues may have improved, there's still a long way to go to restore the company to a level of financial health acceptable to the market. Add this current issue to the number of signs that indicate a fairly serious situation that investors in general should avoid.

Stock Price

Originally, the stock price ran up with the increase in the price of oil. Sometimes a commodity price spike can last long enough for a company like this to reset its balance sheet.

But then the resignations were announced by way of the SEC filing and the stock price dropped as shown above. Until the whole situation cleans up, this could prove to be a very volatile stock.

Income

For the past three years this company has not been very profitable. The lack of profits alone can indicate some rather optimistic profitability assumptions about future projects. If it continues, it points the finger at accounting assumptions that probably need to be revised.

Now there was an impairment charge in the current fiscal year that substantially enlarged the loss. But the lack of profitability in the previous years, as shown above, realistically gives a bit more substance to the impairment charge than would normally be the case. The reason for this is a very logical worry that costs are too high (to cause the low profitability of the two years before that).

Along with the worry that costs are too high is a worry about how projects were both evaluated and then recorded in the accounting system. The impairment charges combined with the fairly low cash flow shown next can point to a bit of optimism in the accounting system for at least some projects or strategies.

This should be compared to the accounting choices made by Warner Bros. Discovery ( WBD) where management repaid significant amounts of debt over three years (compared to the original debt outstanding) from comparatively generous cash flow while reporting losses. It's easy to see the respective lack of cash flow here compared to the debt despite the difference in company sizes.

On top of this, it's very easy to see how the resignation of four board members is coming at a very inopportune time.

Cash Flow

Cash flow would seem to be slightly on the anemic side when compared to the debt.

This is where the latest spike in oil prices could help (even if the company hedged). Those prices just need to hang around long enough to help the company materially.

Free Cash Flow

Free cash flow indicates a serious issue because this company has lately been outspending its cash flow. When this statement is combined with the lack of profits, it would appear that something is seriously wrong.

Note that the third and fourth columns are for the fourth quarter whereas the first two columns of numbers are the annual numbers. The last column is for September.

To me the current situation demonstrates that there's no cash available to repay debt. Note that the cash was spent on other things besides debt repayment to produce negative free cash flow. That's an issue even though the company claims to have lowered its debt in the current fiscal year with a debt swap.

Current Debt

The current debt situation is shown below. However, not included in that debt is some production that was forward sold.

Supposedly debt has improved until the accounting is disclosed for the prepayment agreement.

Along with the debt agreement is the prepayment agreement detailed in the annual report:

Note that the debt has definitely improved by way of the debt swap. But it has also improved because roughly $100 million of the prepayment is not listed as long term debt. Now it's properly disclosed in the financial statements. However, you also have to know where to look. This points to the importance of reading the financial statements completely.

For me, that 100 million should be part of the debt ratio calculation. The official EBITDA was less than $200 million. Adjusted EBITDA was a lot better but still less than $300 million. But to me, that makes the debt ratio calculation a bit on the high side. Typically, Mr. Market wants a debt ratio near 1.0 (the closer, the better.) But Mr. Market may accept a debt ratio as high as 1.5. With that prepayment liability that's not on the books (but is disclosed in the financial statements), the debt ratio seems to be a bit high even after the bond swap.

Conclusion

When I begin to see things like the forward sale of production combined with the resignation of four board members (and still more discussed in the last article), that makes this for me a strong sell. This is not a statement about what may happen with the stock price. Instead, this is more about avoiding dangerous situations, of which this qualifies.

This is an extremely volatile and low-visibility area of the stock market. To me, between the last article and the current article, there are just too many things that point out to me that this is just not for the typical investor.

In fact, with the resignation of the four board members, I would hesitate to evaluate prospects until I hear a clear explanation of literally everything that led up to the resignation and what will change as a result.

Right now, the whole overall company situation indicates the potential for more trouble ahead. Therefore, this remains a strong sell consideration.

Risks

Not including the remaining forward sale in the long-term liability situation is not my personal accounting choice. The reason for this is that the company received more cash than the current liability would lead one to believe. With everything going on, I would want extremely conservative accounting choices (not just allowed accounting choices) as a way of building credibility with the market. Accounting is filled with conservative choices (in really any system in the world) and conservative choices. The research literature indicates the criteria in each case for the conservative choice and why that is.

It should be pointed out that most reorganizations and financial squeezes seem to occur with accounting that's not as conservative as it might be. This shows on the cash flow statement usually as negative free cash flow (consistently) or as low cash flow from operating activities (again, consistently). It's a big area of discussion in the literature.

The latest decline in the stock price indicates at least the beginning of a deterioration in the market's faith in current management. That needs to be reversed quickly before it gathers momentum.

This company has long touted reserves. But I have followed plenty of companies to the hereafter that reported plenty of reserves but inadequate cash flow, as seems to be the case here. Therefore, it would appear that costs need to come down so that cash flow increases at various commodity pricing points.

data.title: Torrid Holdings Is Fit For An Upgrade
data.description:
March 20 was a phenomenal day for shareholders of Torrid Holdings (CURV). Shares of the direct-to-consumer retailer shot up 28% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Although revenue continued declining, and profitability worsened, the fact that sales and earnings per share both came in above what analysts anticipated sent the stock up drastically.

In fact, thanks to this, the stock is now up 69.4% since I reaffirmed it as a "Sell" candidate back in December of last year. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is down 5.3%. At first glance, this might make it seem as though my call on the company was horribly wrong. But in the grand scheme of things, it was actually pretty accurate. You see, since I originally rated it a "Sell" in June of last year, the stock has fallen 66.3%. The market, meanwhile, is up only 8.9% over that time.

This plunge in share price can really be attributed to continued structural weakness for the company. Declining comparable store sales and management's decision to close down underperforming locations sent revenue, profits, and cash flows all down substantially over the last couple of years. In my last article, I acknowledged that the company was still trading at very low multiples. But with management not even willing to provide guidance, bad optics centered around share buyback activities, and rising net debt, I argued that a bearish stance was more appropriate.

But now, with these latest earnings, management has come out with relatively detailed guidance, and data suggests that the worst is now over. We will probably still see revenue fall for the foreseeable future. But on the bottom line, we are seeing signs of growth. This justifies, in my view, upgrading the stock to a "Hold" at this time.

A great quarter, all things considered

After the market closed on March 19, the management team at Torrid Holdings reported financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. The business, which operates as a direct-to-consumer provider of apparel, intimates, and accessories, all dedicated to curvy women, saw its share price skyrocket 28% on March 20 in response to this development. At first glance, this might seem surprising. After all, revenue for the business plummeted from $275.6 million to $236.2 million.

Some of this was because of a 10% drop in comparable store sales. But in addition to this, the company saw the number of locations that it has in operation drop from 634 to 483. In fact, during the quarter on its own, the company closed down 77 locations. The decline in comparable sales came even though the company did see strength in certain categories like dresses. It also saw acceleration in certain sub-brands and a turnaround in its knit tops business. There were some other positive developments as well. Intimates and activewear showed signs of momentum and, according to management, should grow this year. The reintroduction of footwear was also lauded as a success by management. A lot of the weakness when it came to comparable sales was, according to the company, driven by its decision to temporarily pause its shoe business because of tariff-related concerns.

The good news was that even though revenue took such a huge drop, it still came in $5.1 million above what analysts were hoping to see. We also saw outperformance on the bottom line. Earnings per share did deteriorate from negative $0.03 to negative $0.08. But that still ended up being $0.05 greater than what analysts were anticipating. Overall net profits went from negative $3 million to negative $8.1 million. We also saw operating cash flow plunge from $12 million to negative $5.9 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get a fall from $11.8 million to $3.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company declined from $16.9 million to $5.2 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the 2024 and 2025 fiscal years. Revenue, profits, and cash flows all took a hit. The aforementioned decline in the number of locations and operations certainly did a number on the company. But also during this time, it suffered from a 7% drop in comparable store sales. With results like these, you might seem perplexed as to why I'm upgrading the company. My decision to do so has less to do with what has happened in the past and more to do with the future. Management said that even though they intend to close down up to an additional 30 stores by the middle of this year, at that point, their store optimization program would be more or less completed. This, combined with strong growth in key areas such as active wear, intimates, dresses, and more, should help to turn the ship around.

Sub-brands are particularly intriguing to me. In 2025, they accounted for about $70 million of revenue. And for 2026, they expect this to grow to $110 million. This should help to keep sales higher, though overall revenue for 2026 will still drop to between $940 million and $960 million.

More important than that, however, is the belief by management that all of these changes will ultimately prove positive for margins. Cost cutting efforts, especially cutting off money-losing stores, should result in EBITDA this year of between $65 million and $75 million. That compares to the $63.6 million generated in 2025. If we scale adjusted operating cash flow similarly, we would get a reading for it in 2026 of about $69.2 million. This compares favorably to the $62.9 million that the company reported for last year.

If this does truly represent a return to growth for the company, at least from a profitability standpoint, then there is the potential for upside moving forward. After all, even though the stock is up drastically since I last wrote about it, it is trading at incredibly low multiples. Using the estimates that I calculated for 2026 and the historical results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how cheap the stock is compared to five other similar terms. It is cheaper than any of them on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one of these companies was cheaper than it is.

In my previous article, I did talk about rising net debt. From that time through today, we have seen the picture worsen a bit. There has been an increase from $273.7 million to $283 million. But as the chart above illustrates, this still translates to a net leverage ratio that is lower than all but two of the five institutions that I stacked it up against. As long as the company can generate positive cash flow, which shouldn't be so hard considering that it is planning capital expenditures this year of between $8 million and $10 million, then there is a chance for the leverage profile of the company to improve further.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available currently, I do still believe that Torrid Holdings is a risky prospect. Having said that, it has demonstrated that it is clearly in the early stages of a turnaround phase. This justifies some optimism. Given how cheap the stock is, it wouldn't be unthinkable that I would become bullish on it if we see management’s efforts finally start to show through in the form of increased profitability. But for now, I think that upgrading it to just a "Hold" is the right move.

data.title: ZSP:CA - Unhedged S&P 500 ETF Likely To Slide As CAD Appreciates
data.description:
What is ZSP:CA?

The BMO S&P 500 Index ETF (ZSP:CA) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of $20.6 billion CAD that invests in large-cap American listed stocks, with additional minor exposure (under 3%) in global equities outside of the U.S. BMO, and most of the 5 banks, try to provide the same scale of offerings that American fund managers like Vanguard and BlackRock do, and I think they provide cleaner ways to do so in one's portfolio in Canada. BMO's passive offering is very liquid, tracks the S&P 500 very closely, and has a small expense ratio, making it an easy way to get U.S. exposure in Canada. This article seeks to compare this offering with some other larger American fund offerings.

ZSP:CA's benchmark target is the S&P 500 (SPY) the fund has not materially deviated from its benchmark, while also presenting less volatility during selloffs, with a beta of 0.97. VFV:CA or XUS:CA are other similar hedged alternatives, while ZSP:CA is a not. I rate it a Hold because its unhedged, and I anticipate the Canadian dollar improving given oil strength and geopolitical shifts away from U.S. dominance, so the fund would drag in performance.

Fund Breakdown

ZSP:CA was launched on Nov. 14, 2012 by BMO with a plan to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that invested in highly liquid U.S. companies with growth upside. The ETF has successfully tracked the S&P 500 for over 13 years now, showing almost identical returns with a slightly lower Beta.

The fund has an expense ratio of just 0.09%, in line with VFV:CA and slightly above XUS:CA, which is at 0.08%. Additionally, the fund has lots of liquidity, with daily volumes averaging over 146,000 shares traded, which means over $15 million CAD of volume per day. This is an important note, as many funds in Canada, given the lack of institutional and retail ownership, are thinly traded, leading to higher bid/ask spreads and mispricing the ETF units against the underlying holdings. Overall, the structure of the fund is solid and investors have been receptive to buying it to get U.S. large cap exposure.

ZSP:CA is different than other Canadian-listed S&P 500 ETFs because it isn't hedged back to CAD, meaning investors get both U.S. equity returns and exposure to FX currency movements. Unlike hedged alternatives like XSP:CA, ZSP:CA does not use derivatives to hedge exchange rate risk, so returns improve when the U.S. dollar strengthens. Its why the fund has been an under-performer over the past year, and I think it may continue to drag XSP:CA, the hedged version.

With the Canadian economy backed by oil, and with a stable government, I expect CAD to slowly drift towards 1.30, especially with risk that the petro dollar may yield to the yuan. Expanding on that point, my bullish CAD thesis is a structural shift building on the oil trade. WCS should continue to strengthen as Trans-Mountain expands capacity in early 2027 which would reduce the discount and boost income, with higher prices driving higher GDP through producer cash flows. As infrastructure improves and the discount gets smaller, foreign capital should enter into new Canadian builds, like for LNG Canada Phase 2, boosting long-term export capacity. Additionally, diversifying away from the USD should weaken USD demand, particularly if the FED starts moving toward a FED Funds rate of 3%. The Bank of Canada is supported by commodity strength and has had a stable rate around 2.25%-2.50% which is supporting my take on a favorable backdrop for CAD. Therefore, all else equal, I think the unhedged ZSP:CA would continue to underperform a bit versus the hedged options.

Previous Results and Fund Portfolio

When comparing ZSP:CA's portfolio, there are limited differences in the portfolio breakdown to SPY. As of Mar. 18, 2026, the portfolio greatly resembles SPY, with top holdings like Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and sector diversification in line with the benchmark. Ultimately, this fund is almost the same as SPY, which gives investors confidence that the fund's past performance will continue to align with the S&P 500's stocks in the future.

Risks

From a risk perspective, a beta of 0.97 means that the fund will have slightly less volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results. The fund has almost had identical returns, but has seen slight outperformance this year, while lagging the index a little bit over the last year. The ETF has a Sharpe ratio of 1.51, which measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better, though over 1 is good as an investment. Another key risk in investing in this fund is that the S&P 500 slides, which would make the investment depreciate. Recently, the market has seen shaky returns over the past six months, with volatile US trade policy and more uncertainty surrounding the war, and I can't predict what will happen next.

Bottom Line

The bottom line is that this fund is an easy way to get U.S. large cap exposure in Canada. The fund has a low expense ratio that is comparable or better than peers, the fund is very liquid to the tune of $15 million CAD traded per day, and the beta is slightly below the benchmark it tracks. However, I think it will continue to underperform hedged versions as Canadian currency strengthens and therefore, I rate this fund a Hold.

data.title: Super Micro: From Undervalued To Uninvestable
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Just last month, I called Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI) the most misunderstood AI stock in the market and upgraded it to a Strong Buy.

Today, the stock is down over 27%.

Clearly, I was wrong to be bullish.

But what has changed is not the fundamental AI story or even the company’s positioning in the AI infrastructure stack.

What’s changed is trust.

New allegations have resurfaced that a SMCI director was involved in selling illegal chips, Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to China.

The market no longer trusts SMCI, and with good reason. There were plenty of signs, and I chose to ignore them.

It’s a hard lesson to learn, but a lesson learned.

SMCI has become uninvestable in my book.

From Underrated To Uninvestable

In my previous piece, I laid out what I considered to be a logical thesis.

The market was pricing SMCI as a low-margin commodity hardware vendor, while the company was clearly transitioning into a full-stack AI infrastructure provider.

My original thesis was supported by three key pillars:

  • Explosive AI-driven revenue growth

  • Early signs of margin expansion via DCBBS solutions

  • A structural shift toward rack-scale AI factory deployments

None of that has really changed; the problem is that SMCI already had a track record of issues, and that just got much worse.

First, it was the audit of its financials; now, it’s alleged criminal wrongdoing by its co-founder.

The Allegations: Why This Matters

According to a recent report, company insiders, specifically a co-founder and an employee, have been implicated in the smuggling of restricted Nvidia chips. However, it's important to note Super Micro has not been named as a defendant in this indictment.

U.S. officials allege the trio went to great lengths to hide their actions from both U.S.-based server manufacturers and export control authorities, even using hair dryers to remove labels and serial numbers ​from the real machines and placing them on dummy machines left behind after the real machines had been shipped to China.

Source: SA Press Release

This is not a minor issue, especially given the company’s track record.

In today’s geopolitical environment, where AI chips are effectively strategic assets, these are serious allegations.

What’s Next For SMCI?

This is the real question, and the answer is not good.

The stock is facing real governance concerns, and this doesn't seem like something that will get quickly cleared up and cause a sudden reversal.

Even if this is as bad as it gets in terms of negative press, investor confidence has been decimated, and you can no longer value SMCI like a regular company.

More importantly, all eyes are now on customers. This is the real risk.

Any signs of hyperscaler hesitation, delayed orders, or diversification away from SMCI would validate what the market is already beginning to price in.

Longer term, the company must do two things: restore trust and prove margins can scale.

But this seems very difficult right now. It would take a complete upheaval of the company’s management and very, very strong earrings.

But again, the risk is not just the loss of trust by investors; it's the loss of trust by clients.

What Should Investors Do?

I’ve been known to be somewhat of a contrarian, and perhaps this is why I’ve tracked SMCI so closely. However, this is one instance where you don’t want to fight the trend.

I’m happy to take big risks if there’s a big potential payout, but that is no longer the case for SMCI.

The best-case scenario is maybe that somehow the people involved in the chip-smuggling case are cleared.

But even if that happens, you can’t recover the trust of investors.

The delayed financials were strike one. In hindsight, that should have been enough. This is strike two, but I’m not waiting for a third one.

Final Thoughts

I am downgrading SMCI to a Sell, of course, even though the damage has already been done. TA or fundamental analysis won’t do much to help here.

I consider SMCI uninvestable at this point.

With that said, we may very well see days of double-digit gains and short squeezes.

But it’s not something we can reasonably anticipate or invest in.

With thousands of quality, honest companies out there, I see no reason to buy SMCI at this point.

data.title: Target Hospitality Still Aims At Its Potential Core Recovery And Growth Drivers
data.description:
Only two months after the publication of my previous coverage, Target Hospitality Corp. (TH) continued to show its resilience despite the unfavorable things it has encountered in the past year. Its value increased by almost 10%, which justifies my Buy rating, small but continuous. Now, macro headwinds are emerging and appear riskier, but I believe that TH may take advantage of them. Also, fundamentals are still robust with its debt-free Balance Sheet. Valuation and technicals are still in sync and show some upside potential.

TH Q4 2025: Recovery Sustained

In the past year or two, Target Hospitality Corp. has been at its low point due to the contract extinguishment of Pecos Children Center or PCC. But it the second half, TH proved it could rise from every challenge after opening itself to other niches. This paid off as it continued to regain its footing, as shown in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $89.8M, up by 7.3% YoY from $83.7M. This YoY growth was higher than in my previous coverage at only 4.4%, which shows sustained improvement. Services income and specialty retail income weakened. This can tell us that it may still need some time to fully recover from the impact of contract extinguishments. If you look at its revenue per segment, its revenues from government and hospitality services segments fell. However, its construction fees, mainly from workforce hospitality solutions or WHS, brought additional revenues of $32.5M. This also shows that its recovery efforts and strategies have become fruitful as it expanded to other niches.

However, its operating costs and expenses rose. Its cost of services doubled, showing that it was not entirely shielded from inflationary headwinds. As a result, it incurred an operating loss. On a lighter note, one must remember that a huge portion of its costs and expenses were associated with construction service activities as well as the extinguishment of its PCC contract, which used to drive its margins. But for 2026, TH anticipates better margins as its Workforce Hub Contract transitions to a service-focused revenue and higher-margin model. This may be supported by WHS contracts, which are expected to scale and the completion of the Dilley Contract ramp-up phases.

Challenges

Stubborn inflation in the country must still be considered since it affects the budget of companies or groups taking the services and solutions of TH. New tariffs may exacerbate it due to their tendency to raise domestic prices, particularly imports. This may increase the cost pressures of TH since inflation and tariffs may raise the prices of construction materials. The ingredients of the food it serves as part of its housing package may become more expensive. With that, its pricing flexibility and margins may be squeezed.

Middle East Tension: A Double-Edged Sword

The escalation of the tension in Iran sent oil prices back to their 13-month highs. Currently, the prices of Brent (CO1:COM) and WTI (CL1:COM) per barrel are $108.48 and $95.47, respectively. In response, the prices of refined products like fuel may become more expensive. These may have various impacts on TH.

On the negative side, oil price spikes can hurt the economy due to the multiplier effect. Higher oil prices may lead to higher inflation due to higher fuel prices. This may immediately impact shipping, manufacturing, industrial, and logistics. These are large sectors, which may easily affect economic activity. With that, TH may face higher cost pressures on transportation, construction, and day-to-day activities.

On the positive side, higher oil prices may increase upstream activities like oil exploration and production. This usually leads to increased drilling, especially in oil-abundant locations like Texas. So, there may be an influx of demand for worker housing and remote accommodations. TH may take advantage of the situation since it has already been signing contracts with workforces related to energy infrastructure and power plants. With the current range of oil prices, I believe that the sharper side of the sword is more on the positive side. As long as it stays below $120, things may stay good for TH.

Mitigants/Drivers

I understand that the skyrocketing oil prices may have a mixed impact on TH. We cannot yet guarantee that it will purely benefit TH. But at least, TH boasts strengths to protect its business and potentially drive growth.

One aspect to consider is the fact that TH is no longer a pure oil play anymore. For a long time, it focused highly on oil workforce. But in recent years, it has also accommodated other sectors like the government. But what is more interesting is its increased engagement with power infrastructure and data centers. These two are interconnected and may drive growth in TH. Last month, it announced its second bed expansion to data center community. With additional 400 community beds, TH already accommodates up to 1,050 people. This represents a 320% increase from the initial number of 250 community beds. The pace of its bed expansion highlights the increasing momentum of data center capacity demand.

This also shows the crucial role of TH in the continued digital and AI revolution. Recent statistics show that power demand from data centers alone may triple by 2030. Aside from data centers, power infrastructure may increase as well. Both will benefit TH.

These two aspects may also be a growth catalyst for oil and gas drilling activity. Note that natural gas remains the primary electricity source in the U.S. With that, natural gas drilling activities. This may also require more natural gas pipelines and LNG facilities. This increased energy activities can also lead to more workforce being accommodated by TH in the long run.

More importantly, TH maintains its robust fundamentals to support continued expansion. Its cash and cash equivalents may have dropped dramatically to $8.3M. However, it already has a debt-free Balance Sheet. If you look at its equity, the majority of the amount comes from retained earnings. This shows that its net income or operating cash inflow remains enough to sustain its operating capacity. This also suggests that it has enough capital buffer to support its increased activity.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update my DCF Model. Because revenues exceeded my expectations in my previous coverage, it’s not surprising that my new FCFF increased as well. I still set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be consistent with my previous analysis. I still got its WACC using the same method. Its market risk premium is still 7.0% to be conservative and price in more risks than just the standard of 5.0%. Lastly, cash and cash equivalents decreased in line with Q4 2025 results. My new TP or target price is $11.72. This is also higher than the current price and shows that there is still some upside potential at its level.

I also updated its P/B ratio. Currently, the stock trades at 2.33x its book value versus the five-year average of 3.14x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $12.25. This is also quite close to my DCF TP, which proves the reasonability and slight cheapness of the stock.

Technicals

TH’s technicals are still quite bullish despite some selling pressures we have seen recently. It also appears resilient to macroeconomic headwinds today, which can be rooted from the potential advantages of oil prices and the defensive impact of its data center community expansion. The stock price trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA also appears to be shifting upwards, which means that recent market sentiments have started to improve.

Its MACD and RSI also reflect the uptrend, so we cannot detect any bearish divergence. The MACD line is also still above the signal line, which means that buying volume is still increasing. The histogram is within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But the decreasing trend close to zero or neutral warrants some caution because it also suggests that sellers are starting to increase and catch up with buyers. This can be explained by its RSI, which shows that the downtrend was due to the recent overselling that prompted profit-taking. But now that buying room is starting to expand again, the downtrend may become manageable. Investors may also be able to spot an entry point.

Conclusion

Target Hospitality Corp. may still need some time to fully recover, but its data center community expansion and strategic exposure to oil and gas workforces may support it. Robust fundamentals also justify its strength and show enough capital buffer to sustain its increasing capacity. Its valuation is still reasonable and suggests some upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as bullish signals remain evident, with the current momentum normalizing again after the recent overbuying. I’m reiterating my Buy rating on this stock.
